metadata.color_semantics: TEAL = quantum reality (wave, curvature, zero-point energy); CRIMSON = classical expectation (particle at rest, zero energy floor)
metadata.note: Exclusions: no full boundary-value derivation, no normalization constant, no n-squared spectrum table, no uncertainty-principle algebra beyond one plain sentence

beats.narration_text: A particle trapped in a box cannot be brought to rest. Cool it to absolute zero — it still moves. Why?

card.headline: Why a Particle in a Box Cannot Sit Still

beats.narration_text: Draw the energy levels for a particle in a box. The rungs climb upward — and the lowest one is not at the floor. It floats above zero. That gap is the zero-point energy.
Classical reasoning: a particle at rest has zero kinetic energy. Make the box smaller, slow it down more, bring it to a complete stop. Energy should be zero.

card.headline: Classically, a particle at rest has zero energy. Here the ground state always floats above zero. Why can't the bottom rung sit on the floor?

beats.narration_text: Classical picture: shrink the box, slow the particle, remove energy until it stops. Zero energy is reachable. The floor is the floor.
But a trapped particle is a wave. The wavefunction must go to zero at both walls — that is the boundary condition. A wave that hits zero at both ends must fit a whole half-wavelength inside.

card.headline:
Fitting the half-wave
forces curvature — curvature is kinetic energy

beats.narration_text: A flat wave has zero curvature and zero kinetic energy. But a wave that curves — that bends to hit zero at both walls — has curvature. The Schrodinger equation says curvature is kinetic energy. The more curved the wave, the higher the energy.

The ground state fits exactly one half-wavelength. Shrink the box and that half-wave must squeeze tighter — it curves more, so its kinetic energy rises. The energy floor goes up as one over L squared.

This is not a quirk of boxes. Every confined quantum system has a positive energy floor — a zero-point energy. Liquid helium refuses to freeze at absolute zero because the zero-point motion of helium atoms prevents them from locking into a lattice.

props.headline: This is not a quirk of boxes. Every confined quantum system…

beats.narration_text: You cannot remove the zero-point energy by cooling. It is irreducible — not thermal, but structural. The confinement itself is the source.

Box width 0.5 nanometers — roughly an atomic diameter. Ground state energy E-one equals pi-squared h-bar-squared over 2 m L-squared. Plug in the numbers: E-one equals about 1.5 eV. Double the box width: E-one drops by a factor of four, to about 0.37 eV. The energy floor scales as one over L squared — confinement writes the floor.
A particle in a box cannot sit still because confinement forces the wavefunction to curve, and curvature is kinetic energy. The ground state is always above zero. That is the quantum energy floor.

card.headline: Confinement forces curvature. Curvature is energy. The quantum floor cannot be removed.

beats.name: OutroSeries
beats.narration_text: Part of the Quantum Mechanics A Companion Guide series.
